description: This figure presents a quantitative decomposition of yield strength contributions for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steels, comparing Ti/V-free (M1) and Ti/V-modified (M2) alloys in solution-treated (SS) and aged conditions (30 h at 550°C). The bar chart displays measured yield strength values (red bars) alongside calculated contributions from distinct strengthening mechanisms: σ₀ (base strength, blue), σ_G (grain boundary/Hall-Petch, purple), σ_Or (Orowan bypass from (Ti,V)C carbides, green), and σ_S (shear strengthening from κ-carbides, yellow). M1-SS shows minimal precipitation strengthening (~430 MPa), while M1-30 exhibits significant strengthening (~860 MPa) dominated by κ-carbide shearing (~430 MPa). M2-SS demonstrates higher baseline strength (~660 MPa) with Orowan contribution from (Ti,V)C. Notably, M2-30 achieves comparable yield strength (~884 MPa) through a hybrid mechanism: reduced κ-carbide shearing (~220 MPa) supplemented by (Ti,V)C Orowan bypass (~120 MPa). This supports the strategy of using (Ti,V)C as carbon sinks to heterogeneously distribute κ-carbides, optimizing strength while preserving work-hardening capacity.
